MGWVEbct7HU.excerpt: Why some relationships feel frozen in time while others drift — and how to bring an old friendship back up to speed.
MGWVEbct7HU.article:
Have you ever noticed that with some relationships, time seems to stop? You can be apart for years, come back together, and pick up exactly where you left off. With others, even a short gap leaves you feeling like you barely know the person anymore. I want to dig into what actually causes that difference.

Why time passes in some relationships

Your mental clock on a relationship runs on importance. When something more significant than the relationship enters your life, time starts to pass — that connection slips into the background while the bigger thing takes center stage.

Think about the friends you had as a kid. As we grow older, we tend to get more isolated, because everyone heads down their own path and what each person considers important changes. You reconnect with someone you used to talk to about toy trucks and the latest cartoon, but you've both had much weightier things happen since. Your lives have quietly moved in different directions.

When time stands still

Now think about the people you reconnect with and instantly feel at home. That happens when the substance of the relationship is genuinely important — as important, or more important, than anything that fills the time in between. You go about your day-to-day life, you come back together, and you're right back at the same depth you had before. Mentally, no time has passed at all.

Upgrading old relationships

Here's the practical part. If you want to keep an old friendship alive as you get older, the trick is to upgrade it — bring it up to speed with what matters in your adult life. You already have the relationship; you just have to update what it's about. You can reminisce about the old days, but the connection stays alive when you start talking about the things that are important to you now.

This is the easiest way to fight the isolation that creeps in with age. Forming brand-new friendships is hard. Reviving an existing one is much easier, because the foundation is already there.

Finding a shared trajectory

I think of this as shared trajectory. Everyone is moving through life in some direction. You and an old friend might be heading down very different roads, but there's almost always something that overlaps — some pain or some pursuit you still share. Find that obscure point of overlap, latch onto it, and you can bring the relationship current.

It's an interesting lens for understanding why time stops in some relationships and races in others — and a useful one for deciding which old connections are worth bringing back to the level of importance they once had.

uchJP2QGWbo.excerpt: A personal improvement system starts with a simple move: find the pain you've been avoiding, and turn it into a game.
uchJP2QGWbo.article:
I'll probably make a more polished version of this down the road, but I wanted to start sketching out an idea for a personal improvement system. It builds directly on my earlier video about unlocking the why, where I broke down pains and games.

The pain you're neglecting

Once you've defined your pains and your games, a pattern tends to show up: there's usually a pain you're neglecting. It happens because you spend too much time on your games — the things you're naturally good at and find fun — and a genuine pain gets left untouched simply because addressing it isn't as enjoyable.

Turn the pain into a game

The move is to take that neglected pain and turn it into a game. A game has two things going for it: meaningful rewards and a level of difficulty you can actually handle.

So look at the pain and ask how you can make the rewards greater, and how you can break it down into smaller, more playable pieces. A lot of pains stay painful purely because they feel too hard — so you leave them alone and they keep hurting. But once you break a pain into something playable, you can actually start chipping away at it. That's when it begins to make a real difference, because you're finally addressing the things you'd been avoiding.

I'm hoping to develop this further, maybe with some visual walkthroughs that dive into the detail. For now I wanted to get the core idea out there and hear what other people find when they try it.

qnRDoAbbHDM.excerpt: Capture your data now — even when you can't use it yet — because AI is about to make all of it valuable.
qnRDoAbbHDM.article:
As soon as the AI world started arriving, I began thinking seriously about data capture. The logic is simple: you can collect far more data than you could ever analyze by hand, but with AI, all that data suddenly becomes usable — and the more of it you have, the better the insights.

Turning my videos into a data library

These videos are a good example. One of my motivations for making them is data capture. Over time I'm building a database of the concepts and ideas I've worked out. I've automated pulling the transcript from each video, so every one of them becomes searchable text on my site — and text is one of the easiest formats to put to work in all kinds of ways.

Logging more than you think you need

I've done the same with activity tracking. You can pull the raw logs off an activity tracker on your computer and get genuinely interesting insights into your own productivity. There are countless ways to set up tracking and logging like this.

The one caveat: keep it secure. Don't expose anything beyond what you're comfortable with — you don't want bad actors getting hold of your data.

Preparing for the future

The bigger point is about being ready for what's coming. If you start collecting data now, you'll have far more to draw on when you want to take advantage of it later. There are already so many ways to pair AI with data, and that's only going to grow. Capturing it today is a quiet investment in tomorrow.

2vKpOrqkyZo.excerpt: What if your business were an API — or even an MCP server — that customers and their AI agents could query directly?
2vKpOrqkyZo.article:
I've been thinking a lot lately about treating your business like an API. An API is just computers talking to computers, and when you frame a business that way, it gets really interesting.

Your business as a set of answers

Start with the questions your customers ask you. How do you get the answers, and how do you return them? If you can package those answers — placing an order, checking order status, checking availability — into something programmatic, you've essentially built an API for your business that does what a human representative would do.

Once that exists, you can expose it however is convenient. You can integrate directly with your customers, or build a customer portal on your website that runs on top of it. The same underlying answers power every channel.

Getting ready for the agentic world

Now picture the next phase of business, where lots of people are working through AI agents. If you already have this API in place, you're ready for them, and you've made it dramatically easier for customers to do business with you.

Take it a step further: what if your business could simply be an MCP server? A customer connects to your system, generates the key they need to authenticate, and starts sending requests — order something, check status, check availability, and so on. You expose a set of tools covering the questions people commonly ask, and they get instant answers. At that point you're literally ready for the agentic world, which I think is going to be a key advantage for businesses that prepare early.

NKtfqUsn_Cw.excerpt: When AI makes video production cheap, the whole game shifts to storytelling — and whoever masters engineered emotion wins.
NKtfqUsn_Cw.article:
Here's my prediction: the attention economy is going to be won by someone using AI video. Not won exclusively — there will be massive competition — but here's the reasoning behind it.

Production gets cheap, so storytelling becomes everything

AI video makes the production side of video far easier and cheaper. We're not going to suddenly lack the resources to produce. That frees creators to pour all their effort into the part that actually matters: the storytelling, and the engineering of emotion. Someone is going to figure this out and win a huge share of attention without spending much on production at all.

Video tuned to what humans crave

I think of it like food. People came along and engineered foods precisely to the human appetite — sugary, hyper-palatable, hard to put down. The same thing is going to happen with video, especially now that you can make almost any kind of video cheaply. Someone will learn to tune videos to exactly what humans crave. People already do this; it's just going to get a lot more intense.

You can already see it with the Sora app — people generate videos, and people watch them. And here's the thing: it doesn't matter that it's AI. If a video entertains you or makes you feel something, the fact that it isn't real footage doesn't change the experience. A good storyteller makes you feel things, and that works whether the video is real or generated.

There will always be a place for authentic video. But the attention battle is going to be fought on emotion, not on production budgets.

KGjTfJd20y4.excerpt: The famous NASA janitor story is about purpose — but 'go to the moon' is a what, not a why. So what's the real why?
KGjTfJd20y4.article:
Why did we go to the moon? Does anyone actually know why we went to the moon? I've been chewing on this, and it started with a story you've probably heard.

The janitor at NASA

Someone asks a janitor at NASA what he's doing, and instead of saying he's mopping the floor, he says he's helping put a man on the moon. It's a great little story — it's about everyone in an organization understanding the bigger purpose behind their work, rather than just their immediate task. That's genuinely inspiring, and I'm not downplaying it.

A what, not a why

But notice that going to the moon is a what, not a why. It's just a thing you could choose to do. So why go to the moon at all?

My thinking starts from a premise: we do everything in relation to some pain we're trying to solve or avoid. Through that lens, the answer is that we went to the moon to avoid the pain of stagnation. That's a huge force in the human race — an ever-burning desire to reach the next level in the game. That's really what it's all about. We want to play the game and keep advancing. This ties directly into my video on pains and games.

To be clear, there are plenty of other reasons too — all the scientific discoveries that came out of it were real and valuable. I just wanted to clarify the deeper why underneath the what. It's an interesting thing to sit with: why bother going to the moon in the first place?

xyNw8JqX_9E.excerpt: Simon Sinek's golden circle, brought down to the personal level — your why lives where a real pain meets a winnable game.
xyNw8JqX_9E.article:
I'd like to unlock the why for you. The framework here is Simon Sinek's golden circle: every organization knows what it does, some know how they do it, and very few know why they do what they do. I want to bring that down to the personal level, which changes the conversation a bit.

Pains and games

When you go searching for your own why, what you find is pains and games. Both matter. By games, I mean anything you pursue — anything where you can either succeed or fail.

Picture a chart. Along the bottom is the difficulty of the challenge. If something is too easy for where your mind is at, you find it boring, and you don't spend time on boring things. If it's too hard and you feel overwhelmed at the mere thought of it, you drop it. In the middle is the sweet spot: challenging enough to be interesting, but not so hard that you can't succeed.

Where addictions live, and where your why lives

Now add a second axis: how much pain relief the activity actually brings. Take that sweet spot of difficulty and split it. Low pain relief plus high engagement gives you time-waste addictions. A video game is the perfect example — they're engineered to start easy, show you success, then ramp up difficulty exactly in step with your growing ability, so you stay hooked. It's engaging, but it doesn't relieve a real pain.

Find that same level of engagement in something that genuinely matters and relieves a real pain, and that's where you'll find your why.

My own chart

One thing that pains me deeply is watching someone do something manually, over and over. I get an almost emotional reaction — we have to automate this. Happily, AI and automation land right in my sweet spot: hard enough to be a real challenge, but I see enough success that it's a lot of fun. That alignment is exactly where a why shows up.

But here's where I push back on the golden circle a little: one why doesn't encompass your whole identity. I have many pains and games I care about. Another thing that pains me is politics winning out over truth — and all sides are guilty of it. I understand why politics happens, and it's largely outside my circle of influence, so I choose not to play. That one sits on the far-too-hard end of the chart. The lesson: if you play a game you're not positioned to win, you lose. Choose your games wisely.

Surface-level society

One more pain: surface-level society — the whole internet culture of likes, followers, and shallow metrics. What it breeds is shallow relationships. Someone in your comments section isn't really a friend.

So here's a small game I'll try to play. If this idea meant something to you, share it with someone, tell them why you found it interesting, and have a real conversation about it. That's a tiny bit of influence on the surface-level culture — and I'd rather play that game than the metrics one.

tKmJP-OEXGU.excerpt: A quick automation win: a scheduled email that researches local events and discounts and delivers them to my inbox.
tKmJP-OEXGU.article:
I wanted to share a small automation I put together recently. Part of the goal was just to try out Gumloop as an automation platform — they have some solid connectors. I won't sing their praises to the treetops, but I did build one genuinely useful thing with it.

An email that researches itself

I set up an automated email to myself. It researches the web and then sends me an update on local events, discounts, and other things going on in my area. It's actually made a difference — I've been tipped off to interesting things happening nearby that I'd never have known about otherwise.

It's a simple idea, but a nice one, and it's easy enough that you could try something similar yourself if it sounds useful.

-YpRol8m5QQ.excerpt: An unpopular opinion: keeping up with AI isn't that hard, as long as you stop trying to try every single tool.
-YpRol8m5QQ.article:
Here's an unpopular opinion: you can keep up with AI, and it's not even that hard. Let me give some context, because the usual story is the opposite — there's so much new AI coming out that nobody can keep up.

You can't try everything — and you don't need to

Is there some tool out there that could help me do my job better than I do it now? Of course. There are tons, and I haven't tried them all, and I never will, because if I tried every tool I'd spend all my time trying tools. It's like trying to watch all of YouTube — more hours get uploaded every day than exist in a day. That's just the reality.

The significant stuff always reaches the news

The good news is that anything truly significant hits the news. If you follow a few main AI channels, the important updates will find you. ChatGPT is a great example — I saw the word for probably a month before I bothered clicking on it, because the headlines meant nothing to me at first. Eventually I clicked, realized this was going to change everything, and caught up just fine.

It happened again with the wave of agent tools. I saw the buzz, was a few days late, looked into it, and decided some of it was a security nightmare and steered clear. The point is: I evaluated it on my own timeline and didn't miss anything that mattered.

Being a month late won't sink you

If something is significant enough that all your competitors will adopt it and leave you behind, it's going to be everywhere — everyone will be talking about it. Finding out about a tool a month late doesn't cost you some decisive edge. Customers don't switch who they buy from just because a competitor adopted a new tool a few weeks earlier; that's not how it works.

So yes, there's truth to the fear of falling behind — but only if you ignore AI entirely. If you stay mildly interested, keep an eye on the big updates, and try new things now and then, you'll be fine. Just check the major news, understand roughly what's going on, and you'll catch the big shifts when they come.

HoGdiOisQJE.excerpt: Three spring-cleaning tricks — including the one nobody talks about: using up the things you're 'saving for later.'
HoGdiOisQJE.article:
I was doing some spring cleaning recently and figured out a few tips worth sharing.

Move everything out

When you're clearing out a space — say, a drawer — move everything in it to another spot first. Once it's all displaced, the garbage becomes glaringly obvious. You'll spot things you completely forgot existed and that are clearly junk. Emptying the space is what makes it easy.

Digitize

This one's well known, but worth repeating. There are plenty of good apps that let you scan a document, turn it into a PDF, and upload it to the cloud. All those stacks of paper can simply become files.

Use what you're saving

This is the most interesting one: consumables that have fallen out of the stream of consumption. Think of the things in your freezer you forgot about — food you mean to eat but have no actual date for, so it ends up tossed. Or the fancy gift item you're saving for a special occasion that never seems to arrive.

So here's your inspiration: today is a special day. Start using up the things you've been saving for some vague someday — the nice candle, the special treat, whatever it is. If it's genuinely earmarked for a date on the calendar, fine. But if it's just tucked away for an occasion that doesn't exist, get it out and use it. Hope it helps your spring cleaning.

f4H4cPeB-UM.excerpt: Kids give brutally honest feedback — then learn to filter it. What do we lose when we stop telling each other the truth?
f4H4cPeB-UM.article:
Here's a concept I've been turning over: what if everyone could take brutal feedback and just be fine with it? Go ahead, bash me over the head — no hard feelings. It's an interesting thing to imagine.

How we learn to filter

I started thinking about this after seeing something about how kids give brutally honest feedback. It's true — kids don't yet have the social awareness that what they say might hurt someone. But somewhere along the way, children learn the impact of their words, realize they might hurt someone's feelings, and start holding back. We filter out all that honest feedback we could be giving — and the result is that we stop getting the insight into ourselves that we probably really need.

Why the filter exists

There's obviously a reason it works this way. If we felt nothing but harsh truths about ourselves all the time, we wouldn't perform well — we'd just get discouraged. So the filtering protects us. But it comes at a cost.

The feedback loop

I think about feedback loops constantly, especially with automation: you watch where the automation failed, tweak it, and it gets better and better. The same principle applies to everything human. Getting good feedback is essential to becoming the best version of yourself. So the question that fascinates me is: how do we get the level of honest feedback we need to actually grow — without the hard feelings shutting it down?

r_ecyy5VXj4.excerpt: A quick update on my video pipeline: drop a file in a folder, and it uploads, posts, transcribes, and becomes searchable.
r_ecyy5VXj4.article:
Just a quick update on the automation I've set up for video uploads. I've added another step to the pipeline.

From folder to searchable transcript

It already worked like this: I drop a video into a folder, and it automatically uploads and gets posted to my website. The new step is transcription. After it posts, an API call goes out to fetch the video's transcript, and that transcript gets posted to the site too.

The result is a search bar where you can search across the transcripts of every video I publish. I'm pretty excited about it — now all the text from all my videos is on the site and fully searchable.

This is the kind of automation I love playing with: seeing how little work I can do for how much value.

oRjZ1WQjigs.excerpt: After trying custom GPTs, skills, and every tool around, I keep coming back to plain chat — and there's a lesson in that.
oRjZ1WQjigs.article:
Something I've found interesting: I've tried a lot of things — custom GPTs, skills, all sorts of AI tools — and I keep finding myself going back to just plain chat. Often even the fast, no-thinking model handles the question fine.

Don't automate too early

There's no universal right answer here — some tasks genuinely need reasoning or thinking capabilities. But what I take from it is that I've sometimes tried to automate something before it actually needed automating. The instinct is, let's build a custom tool just for this — when really, plain chat would have worked perfectly well.

Do it manually first

This loops back to a rule I keep landing on: do it manually first. That's my key rule for automation. When you do something by hand, you deeply understand how it works, and that's exactly what tells you what it'll take to automate it well.

I tend to jump to automating everything before it's ready. So a takeaway for myself: wait until I've seen the same repetition three or four times before building a special tool. Once I'm clearly doing the exact same thing over and over, it makes sense. I'm just a little too jumpy to automate — but honestly, it's a lot of fun.

SVwk9nLQECc.excerpt: The viral ChatGPT stopwatch fail wasn't really about a missing tool — it's about agents that don't know their own limits.
SVwk9nLQECc.article:
In the age of AI, most of my ideas take about an afternoon to pull off. The idea of dropping a video into a folder and having it automatically appear on my website has taken two afternoons — a big project, apparently, by modern standards. We'll see if this one shows up as expected.

The stopwatch that lied

I want to talk about a video that's been circulating. Someone asked ChatGPT Voice to start a stopwatch, and it said it had. They told it to stop, and it reported that seven minutes had passed — when it had really been about ten seconds. It was simply making the number up. I was able to reproduce the exact same issue.

Sam Altman responded to the clip and framed it as a missing tool: ChatGPT Voice doesn't have a stopwatch, and that feature is about a year out.

The real problem: agents that don't know their limits

I think about it differently, because I'm in the middle of designing agents and I hit this exact problem. An agent will say, sure, I can do that — and then go ahead and pretend it can, even when it can't. In my case it would gather a bunch of information, and only fail right at the end, because it never knew it wasn't actually capable of the task.

The missing tool isn't really the issue. The issue is self-awareness. The fix is to tell your model up front what it doesn't have — so it stops accepting requests for tools it doesn't actually possess.

This is incredibly relevant to what I do. We have to make agents understand their own capabilities so they don't mislead and frustrate users. User experience is everything when designing these tools.

TMyqjOFRVEc.excerpt: The first post — a deliberately plain test to prove an automated YouTube upload pipeline actually works.
TMyqjOFRVEc.article:
Hello world — what a technical pun.

This video has nothing to do with being fancy or getting views. It exists to prove one thing: that an automated workflow for uploading a video to YouTube actually works.

The experiment

If this shows up on my channel, then the first hello-world experiment was a success. That's the whole point.

My theory is that uploading new videos should be very easy — I'm going to try to automate the entire process so that publishing is as simple as dragging a file into a folder. This is the first test of that idea.
